results.possibleIdentity: Mary Jones is an American painter and art critic who began her career in Los Angeles and has been based in New York City since 1986. She was active in the Los Angeles art scene during the 1970s and is included in the encyclopedia "L.A. Rising: SoCal Artists Before 1980," which documents influential artists in Southern California prior to the 1980s. Her work often explores the intersection of traditional painting and modern technology, such as her series "Significant Properties," which utilizes oil on digitally printed canvas. Jones has a significant historical association with the discourse surrounding Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.); archival records at the Hirshhorn Museum and Sculpture Garden include an artist file for her located in the same collection block as records for E.A.T. Furthermore, as an art writer and critic for publications like BOMB Magazine and the Brooklyn Rail, she has extensively documented the history of art-and-technology collaborations, including a noted 2012 interview with performance artist Barbara T. Smith regarding the 1971 Art and Technology exhibition at LACMA. She has served as a Senior Critic at the Rhode Island School of Design (RISD) since 1998 and an instructor at the School of Visual Arts (SVA) in New York since 2009.

A potential namesake or benefactor connection exists via Mary Jones Horning (often referred to as Mary Jones in institutional titles), who, along with Thomas Hart Horning, endowed positions specifically dedicated to the intersection of art, science, and technology. This includes the "Mary Jones and Thomas Hart Horning Chief Curator of Art, Science and Technology" at the Patricia Valian Reser Center for the Creative Arts (PRAx) at Oregon State University. This position is explicitly tasked with promoting the same interdisciplinary collaborations—between artists, engineers, and scientists—that were the hallmark of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization.

results.possibleIdentity: Douglas K. Smith (1945–2010) was a multi-talented professional who embodied the intersection of technical engineering and fine art, a profile consistent with the goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He was a retired mechanical and nuclear engineer who worked for the Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) and also served as a helicopter pilot for the presidential detail under Lyndon B. Johnson. Parallel to his engineering career, he was an active local artist and photographer, beginning his career in the late 1960s and early 1970s. His diverse interests included archaeology, sailing, and nature, often capturing these themes in his artistic work.

Douglas C. Smith is an electrical engineer who was a Distinguished Member of Technical Staff at AT&T/Bell Laboratories, the institution where Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) was founded. Active during the late 1960s and 1970s, Smith holds numerous patents and is a specialist in high-frequency measurements, circuit/system design, and noise in electronic circuits. Given E.A.T.'s reliance on Bell Labs engineers to provide technical support for artistic collaborations (such as '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering'), he represents a likely technical identity associated with the organization's collaborative projects.

Douglas Smith is identified as a composer and artist in the papers of Charles Shere, a prominent San Francisco Bay Area art and music critic active in the late 1960s and 1970s. Smith is listed alongside other avant-garde figures in records that specifically document the activities of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in both the Bay Area and New York. This suggests he was part of the experimental music or technical-art scene that E.A.T. fostered during its peak years of activity.

Douglas Smith is an American illustrator who began his career in the 1970s. He studied illustration at the Rhode Island School of Design (RISD), graduating in 1974. During the early 1970s, he worked as a freelance artist for publications such as the Boston Globe and Boston Magazine and began experimenting with scratchboard art, a medium for which he would later become famous. He is well-known for illustrating Gregory Maguire's novel 'Wicked' and has a long history of creating art for environmental and commercial causes.

results.possibleIdentity: James E. Kennedy is a physicist and engineer who was active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a nonprofit organization that facilitated collaborations between artists and engineers. Kennedy worked at Sandia National Laboratories in Albuquerque, New Mexico, and the IIT Research Institute in Chicago, where he became a prominent expert in the physics of explosives and pyrotechnics (notably developing the Gurney model). Within the E.A.T. community, he served as a technical collaborator, and his work is listed in the definitive 1972 publication 'Pavilion' by Billy Klüver, which documents the major E.A.T. project for the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka. Beyond his engineering career, Kennedy has a documented interest in the humanities and art; in the early 1970s, he taught courses in African-American art and African art at the University of South Alabama. He also pursued research in parapsychology, starting at the Institute for Parapsychology (founded by J. B. Rhine), where he published numerous papers investigating the 'Association Between Anomalous Experiences and Artistic Creativity and Spirituality.'

results.possibleIdentity: Gloria (Eksergian) Shaw (1921–2002) was a dancer, choreographer, and designer active in the mid-20th century. Based in Vermont, she and her husband, Gilbert Shaw (an inventor and businessman), collaborated on several architectural and industrial design projects during the 1950s and 1960s. Notably, they designed the distinctive buildings for the Polymers Company on Route 116 in Middlebury, Vermont. She was also a prominent figure in the regional arts community, serving as a choreographer for the Middlebury Community Players and as an early board member of the Vermont Mozart Festival. Her background was deeply rooted in both art and engineering; her grandfather, Carnig Eksergian, was a portrait painter, while her father and uncle were MIT graduates known for their engineering and design work on locomotives and cannons. Her professional life blended artistic expression with industrial and architectural design, a focus that aligns with the cross-disciplinary goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

Gloria J. Shaw is a contemporary painter and printmaker, currently active on the Monterey Peninsula in California and previously associated with the Vancouver art scene. Born around 1943, she began her career in the medical research sciences, which significantly influenced her artistic practice. She is known for using a 'science background' to experiment with various materials and methods, particularly in the creation of fine art monotypes and mixed media paintings. Her work often emphasizes color and textural influences, and she has described her evolution as a 'trilogy of techniques.' She has been an active member of the Federation of Canadian Artists, the North Shore Artists Guild, and the Dundarave Print Workshop. In the late 1990s, she was involved in environmental art projects, such as 'Friends, Artists, and Neighbors of Elkhorn Slough,' using art to advocate for land conservation.

results.possibleIdentity: Betty Voelker (later known as the artist Hera) was a conceptual, environmental, and performance artist active in the late 1960s and 1970s. She worked under the name Betty Voelker until 1977, after which she began exploring "tent forms" and adopted the name Hera. She is known for large-scale structural and environmental installations, most notably "Passageways" (1973) at the Fort Worth Art Center Museum, which featured an inflated vinyl and polyethylene sculpture forming a 100-foot-long tunnel. Her work often utilized industrial materials and focused on themes of enclosure and structural frameworks. Voelker had an association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, a connection reinforced by her focus on technical and environmental art. Her work is documented in several prestigious collections, including the Giuseppe Panza Papers (Minimal and Conceptual Art) at the Getty Research Institute and the Political Art Documentation/Distribution (PAD/D) Archive at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA).

Elizabeth "Betty" Voelker (1931–2000) was an internationally recognized artist and colorist originally from Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. She graduated with a B.F.A. in 1953 from the Carnegie Institute of Technology (now Carnegie Mellon University), where she studied under Samuel Rosenberg. In 1958, she moved to San Francisco, where she became active as an artist and a lecturer for museums, including the University of California, from 1972 to 1987. Her artistic output consisted primarily of abstract nature paintings and formal abstracted collages. Her work is held in permanent collections at the National Museum of American Art in Washington, D.C., the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art (SFMOMA), the Santa Barbara Museum of Art, and the Carnegie Institute Art Museum. While well-documented as a fine artist in the late 1960s and 1970s, her work in traditional painting and collage is distinct from the conceptual/structural work more commonly associated with E.A.T. collaborations.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert O'Brien (born 1939) is a British-born artist and educator who has been based in Hong Kong since 1976. He was active in the London art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s, having graduated from the Central School of Art and Design (now Central Saint Martins) in London with a degree in Theater Design Technology. Before his formal education, he worked as an apprentice scene painter at the Drury Lane Theatre. In the mid-1960s, he began holding solo and group exhibitions in the United Kingdom, France, and West Germany. After moving to Hong Kong, he became a prominent figure in the local art community, teaching at the Extra-Mural Department of the University of Hong Kong and the Hong Kong Art School. His work often explores theater design and painting, and he has held numerous solo exhibitions, including 'Transformation' (1996) and 'Woods for the Trees' (2002). His background in 'Theater Design Technology' aligns with the multidisciplinary and technical collaborative nature of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization.

Robert A. O'Brien, Jr. was a scientist and engineer active in the 1970s who may be the individual associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in a technical capacity. He served as a member of the Shipboard Computing Group (Code 8003) at the Naval Research Laboratory (NRL) in Washington, D.C. His work involved the development of computer programs for marine science and bathymetric chart digitization. Records from the 1970s indicate he utilized and adapted technical software originally developed at Bell Laboratories, the institution where E.A.T. founders Billy Klüver and Fred Waldhauer were based. Given E.A.T.'s primary mission to foster collaborations between artists and engineers/scientists, O'Brien's technical background and Bell Labs-adjacent research place him within the professional network that supported the organization's activities during the late 1960s and 1970s.

results.possibleIdentity: Sari Dienes (1898–1992) was a Hungarian-born American experimental artist who was active in the New York avant-garde scene from the 1940s through the early 1990s. She is highly likely to be the artist referred to as 'Sari Dienno' due to her documented association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s. Dienes is specifically cited in the E.A.T. records at the Getty Research Institute for her project proposals, which included five photographs and related correspondence. Her connection to E.A.T. was further cemented by her close friendship with its co-founder Robert Rauschenberg; the composer John Cage, another E.A.T. participant, famously requested in 1968 that funds owed to him by the organization be redirected to her. Professionally, Dienes was a multidisciplinary artist known for her 'Sidewalk Rubbings' (frottage) of manhole covers and urban textures, as well as work in assemblage, ceramics, and sound-art installations. She was a founding member of the A.I.R. Gallery, the first women's cooperative art gallery in the U.S., in the early 1970s. Her studio at the Sherwood Studios and her later residence at the James Brown House (home of the Ear Inn) served as influential hubs for artists like Jasper Johns and Robert Rauschenberg.

results.possibleIdentity: Oliver Selfridge (1926–2008), often referred to as 'Olivier' in French contexts, was a pioneering British-American computer scientist and a key figure in the early history of artificial intelligence. While primarily known for his scientific work, such as the 'Pandemonium' model of pattern recognition (1959), he was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s as a collaborator and theorist within the intersection of art and technology. He is notably associated with the discourse of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and the 'Systems Esthetics' movement. Selfridge collaborated with the prominent art theorist Jack Burnham, with whom he and Jack Nolan worked on an influential essay titled 'The Aesthetics of Intelligent Systems,' published in 1970. This work explored the potential of computers in art-making and the development of intelligent artistic systems. During this period, he was a frequent figure in the interdisciplinary community at MIT’s Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS), where engineers and scientists engaged with artists to promote the goals of organizations like E.A.T. His name appears in historical accounts of algorithmic and computer art, often cited for his early work on machine perception which provided a theoretical foundation for artists experimenting with computer-generated imagery and autonomous systems.

results.possibleIdentity: John "Nelson" Richardson (1947–2005) was an American artist and educator active from the late 1960s through the early 2000s. Born in Santa Monica, California, he was a Vietnam War veteran and a graduate of Humboldt State University. Richardson was a pioneer in the intersection of art and technology, founding the computer graphic arts program at Bakersfield College in California, where he served as a professor. His artistic practice focused on painting and graphic arts, specifically exploring optical illusions, pointillism, and abstract landscapes. His work from the 1970s, such as "When Values Meet" (1975) and "No. 4 (Searchlight Illusion)," utilized kaleidoscopic structures to explore perception. Later works included "Christo Reaction" (1991), an acrylic-on-panel homage to the large-scale environmental installations of Christo and Jeanne-Claude. Richardson's career was characterized by a lifelong interest in the visual sensory system and the use of technology to enhance artistic expression, which aligns with the mission of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. He spent significant time in New York City, where he was noted for his imaginative approach to art and books.
